questions.prompt: What is a common role of a load balancer?

answers.text: Health checks can prevent routing to unhealthy instances.

feedback.legit: Health checks can prevent routing to unhealthy instances. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Health checks can prevent routing to unhealthy instances. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: Distribute incoming requests across healthy service instances.

feedback.legit: Distribute incoming requests across healthy service instances. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Distribute incoming requests across healthy service instances. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: A load balancer routes traffic; it is not the primary database.

feedback.legit: A load balancer routes traffic; it is not the primary database. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: A load balancer routes traffic; it is not the primary database. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

questions.prompt: Why is a cache placed in front of a slower data source?

answers.text: To serve repeated reads with lower latency and less backend load.

feedback.legit: To serve repeated reads with lower latency and less backend load. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: To serve repeated reads with lower latency and less backend load. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: To guarantee that data can never become stale.

feedback.legit: Caches need an explicit freshness and invalidation strategy. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: Caches need an explicit freshness and invalidation strategy. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: A cache miss still requires access to the underlying source.

feedback.legit: A cache miss still requires access to the underlying source. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: A cache miss still requires access to the underlying source. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Automatic protection from every application bug.

feedback.legit: A bad write can be replicated to every copy. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: A bad write can be replicated to every copy. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: A live replica is a complete substitute for an independent backup.

feedback.legit: Replication can copy deletion or corruption, so recovery still needs an appropriate backup strategy. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Replication can copy deletion or corruption, so recovery still needs an appropriate backup strategy. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Additional copies that can improve availability or read capacity.

feedback.legit: Additional copies that can improve availability or read capacity. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Additional copies that can improve availability or read capacity. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: Cross-shard queries and transactions can become more complex.

feedback.legit: Cross-shard queries and transactions can become more complex. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Cross-shard queries and transactions can become more complex. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: It partitions data across multiple database nodes.

feedback.legit: It partitions data across multiple database nodes. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: It partitions data across multiple database nodes. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: The partition key determines placement and strongly affects balance.

feedback.legit: The partition key determines placement and strongly affects balance. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: The partition key determines placement and strongly affects balance. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: Why are idempotency keys useful for write APIs?

answers.text: They help repeated delivery avoid creating the same logical operation twice.

feedback.legit: They help repeated delivery avoid creating the same logical operation twice. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: They help repeated delivery avoid creating the same logical operation twice. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: They make network retries unnecessary.

feedback.legit: Retries still occur; the key helps the server recognize duplicates. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Retries still occur; the key helps the server recognize duplicates. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: The server must store or derive the result associated with a key.

feedback.legit: The server must store or derive the result associated with a key. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: The server must store or derive the result associated with a key. This was accurate as written.

questions.prompt: What does a durable message queue help decouple?

answers.text: It guarantees each message is processed exactly once without application logic.

feedback.legit: Many queues use at-least-once delivery, so consumers should handle duplicates. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Many queues use at-least-once delivery, so consumers should handle duplicates. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: A queue always preserves one global message order across all consumers.

feedback.legit: Ordering guarantees depend on the queue type, partitioning, and consumer design. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Ordering guarantees depend on the queue type, partitioning, and consumer design. That was correctly classified as Sus.

answers.text: It lets producers and consumers operate at different rates and tolerate each other's temporary outages.

feedback.legit: It lets producers and consumers operate at different rates and tolerate each other's temporary outages. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: It lets producers and consumers operate at different rates and tolerate each other's temporary outages. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of a circuit breaker?

answers.text: A half-open state can probe whether recovery has occurred.

feedback.legit: A half-open state can probe whether recovery has occurred. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: A half-open state can probe whether recovery has occurred. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: Stop repeated calls to a failing dependency for a period.

feedback.legit: Stop repeated calls to a failing dependency for a period. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Stop repeated calls to a failing dependency for a period. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: Repair the dependency automatically.

feedback.legit: It limits cascading damage but does not fix the underlying service. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: It limits cascading damage but does not fix the underlying service. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Capacity and fairness by restricting request volume over time.

feedback.legit: Capacity and fairness by restricting request volume over time. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Capacity and fairness by restricting request volume over time. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: It guarantees low latency after every accepted request.

feedback.legit: Accepted work can still be slow for other reasons. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Accepted work can still be slow for other reasons. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: Distributed rate limits require a strategy for shared state or approximation.

feedback.legit: Distributed rate limits require a strategy for shared state or approximation. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Distributed rate limits require a strategy for shared state or approximation. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: Why serve static assets through a CDN?

answers.text: A CDN makes cache invalidation unnecessary.

feedback.legit: Changed content still needs versioning, expiry, or invalidation. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Changed content still needs versioning, expiry, or invalidation. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: A CDN safely caches every origin response by default.

feedback.legit: Cacheability depends on method, headers, status, and CDN configuration; private responses need particular care. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: Cacheability depends on method, headers, status, and CDN configuration; private responses need particular care. That was correctly classified as Sus.

answers.text: Edge locations can reduce latency and origin traffic.

feedback.legit: Edge locations can reduce latency and origin traffic. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Edge locations can reduce latency and origin traffic. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

questions.prompt: What choice does the CAP theorem highlight during a network partition?

answers.text: Different operations can make different tradeoffs.

feedback.legit: Different operations can make different tradeoffs. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Different operations can make different tradeoffs. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: A distributed system cannot guarantee both linearizable consistency and availability for every request while the partition persists.

feedback.legit: A distributed system cannot guarantee both linearizable consistency and availability for every request while the partition persists. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: A distributed system cannot guarantee both linearizable consistency and availability for every request while the partition persists. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: A system must permanently choose only two of three properties even without failures.

feedback.legit: The tradeoff concerns behavior while a partition prevents communication. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: The tradeoff concerns behavior while a partition prevents communication. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

questions.prompt: What is backpressure in a data-processing system?

answers.text: A way for slower consumers to limit or signal upstream production.

feedback.legit: A way for slower consumers to limit or signal upstream production. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: A way for slower consumers to limit or signal upstream production. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: A requirement to buffer unlimited work in memory.

feedback.legit: Unlimited buffering delays failure and can exhaust resources. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Unlimited buffering delays failure and can exhaust resources. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: Dropping, batching, blocking, or shedding work are possible policies.

feedback.legit: Dropping, batching, blocking, or shedding work are possible policies. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: Dropping, batching, blocking, or shedding work are possible policies. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: A single ACID transaction that locks every service database.

feedback.legit: Sagas avoid one global database transaction across services. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Sagas avoid one global database transaction across services. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: A compensating action automatically rewinds database history as if the earlier step never happened.

feedback.legit: Compensation is new business logic and may not perfectly reverse every prior effect. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Compensation is new business logic and may not perfectly reverse every prior effect. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: A saga guarantees globally serializable isolation across every participating service.

feedback.legit: A saga coordinates local transactions but does not by itself provide one globally serializable transaction. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: A saga coordinates local transactions but does not by itself provide one globally serializable transaction. That makes your Sus choice accurate.
